text: The surgeon begins dissection on the right lateral peritoneum at the level of the sacral promontory, opening the peritoneum along the pelvis. The dissection progresses into the rectovaginal septum, with a vaginal retractor introduced to enhance exposure.

The surgeon uses sharp and blunt dissection to reach the distal part of the rectum on both sides, exposing the levator ani muscle at the upper part of the anal canal. The dissection is approximately two to three centimeters above the dentate line, confirmed by digital rectal examination.

During the dissection of the rectovaginal septum, the surgeon applies traction and countertraction, leaving fatty tissue on the posterior surface of the vagina to create an extra protective layer against mesh erosion. The lateral stalks of the rectum and the posterior surface of the mesorectum are not dissected, allowing the rectum to remain positioned in the presacral space.

A non-resorbable L-shaped Marcelin mesh is cut and applied anteriorly over the rectum, with its long limb positioned on the right side. The mesh is interposed between the rectum and vagina, and the lower end is sutured to the levator muscles on either side of the rectum, avoiding direct attachment to the rectum to prevent mesh erosion.

The surgeon dissects an avascular area over the sacral promontory and anterior prevertebral ligament. The Marcelin mesh is then fixed to the sacral promontory using one or two stitches, ensuring there is no tension on the mesh.

The surgeon places an additional suture to secure the upper part of the vagina to the mesh, effectively obliterating the Douglas pouch. Subsequently, the peritoneum is closed over the mesh, completing the procedure.
